text:
COMBINED IMAGING REPORT
SURVEILLANCE CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND BILATERAL BREAST MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

PATIENT: Maria Elena Reyes
INDICATION: 46-year-old woman with history of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, triple-negative subtype, Grade 3, originally staged cT2N1M0, treated with neoadjuvant pembrolizumab plus dose-dense AC followed by weekly paclitaxel, left partial mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection (ypT1cN1, RCB-II, non-pCR), adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab maintenance (now completed), and adjuvant radiation therapy to left breast with regional nodal irradiation. Presenting now for end-of-treatment surveillance imaging at approximately 12 months post-surgery, coinciding with completion of all planned adjuvant systemic therapy.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis dated approximately 6 months post-surgery; bilateral breast MRI dated approximately 12 months prior (post-neoadjuvant, pre-surgery baseline and post-operative 12-month comparisons where available).

---

PART I: CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of intravenous iodinated contrast material in the portal venous phase. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reconstructions reviewed. Bone windows and lung windows reviewed in detail.

CT CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well expanded. There are no new pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidations. No endobronchial lesions. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. There is a subtle area of mild ground-glass opacity and mild linear atelectatic change in the left lower lobe posteriorly, measuring approximately 2.1 x 1.4 cm in greatest cross-sectional dimension. This finding is stable in appearance compared to the prior CT obtained approximately 6 months ago, and in the context of prior radiation to the left chest wall and regional nodal basins, this is consistent with evolving/resolving post-radiation fibrotic and inflammatory change. No new ground-glass or interstitial abnormality is seen elsewhere. The right lung is clear.

Pleura: No pleural effusions bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening beyond what was previously noted on the left, which remains stable and is felt to be post-radiation in etiology.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is not widened. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes by size criteria. The prevascular, paratracheal, subcarinal, and para-esophageal nodal stations are all unremarkable. No hilar lymphadenopathy. The heart is normal in size. There is no pericardial effusion.

Breast Tissue / Chest Wall: The residual left breast parenchyma demonstrates expected post-lumpectomy and post-radiation architectural distortion in the upper outer quadrant, consistent with prior surgical and radiation changes. A small metallic clip artifact is identified in this region, corresponding to the known tumor bed localization clip. No suspicious focal soft tissue mass or abnormal enhancement pattern is identified within the breast parenchyma on this contrast-enhanced CT (noting the MRI provides more definitive soft tissue characterization, detailed below). There is no skin thickening beyond expected post-radiation change. The left pectoralis major and minor muscles are intact. No chest wall invasion.

Axillae: The left axillary surgical bed shows expected post-operative fibrotic stranding and soft tissue changes, unchanged from the prior study. No new or enlarged left axillary lymph nodes are identified. The largest residual left axillary node measures approximately 0.8 cm in short axis and demonstrates a reconstituted fatty hilum, morphologically benign-appearing. No right axillary lymphadenopathy.

Supraclavicular and Infraclavicular Regions: No pathologically enlarged nodes in the bilateral supraclavicular, infraclavicular, or internal mammary nodal chains. There is mild stranding in the left supraclavicular region, stable and consistent with post-radiation change.

CT ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with a smooth contour. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous attenuation without focal hypodense or hyperdense lesions in the pre-contrast phase. Following contrast administration, there is uniform hepatic enhancement without focal arterial enhancing lesions, without peripheral washout, and without capsular retraction to suggest metastatic disease. No biliary ductal dilation. The gallbladder is present, non-distended, without calculi or wall thickening.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances uniformly. The pancreatic duct is not dilated. No peripancreatic stranding or mass.

Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal masses or nodules.

Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric nephrographic enhancement without focal cortical lesions. No hydronephrosis. No perirenal stranding.

Retroperitoneum: No enlarged retroperitoneal, para-aortic, or aortocaval lymph nodes. No retroperitoneal mass. The aorta is normal in caliber.

Bowel: No bowel wall thickening, obstruction, or mesenteric mass. No free intraperitoneal air. No ascites.

CT PELVIS:

Pelvic Viscera: The uterus is normal in size and configuration. No adnexal masses or ovarian lesions are identified. The bladder is adequately distended and unremarkable.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy by size criteria.

Pelvic Bones and Soft Tissues: No suspicious pelvic soft tissue mass.

SKELETAL SURVEY (Bone Windows, Entire Study):

Careful review of all visualized osseous structures, including the thoracic and lumbar spine, ribs, sternum, clavicles, pelvis, proximal femora, and scapulae, shows no lytic lesions, blastic lesions, or cortical destruction to suggest osseous metastatic involvement. There is no pathologic fracture. No suspicious periosteal reaction. Mild degenerative facet changes are noted in the lower lumbar spine, age-appropriate.

CT IMPRESSION:
No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Specifically:
- No pulmonary metastases
- No hepatic metastases
- No suspicious lymphadenopathy in any nodal station
- No osseous metastatic lesions
- Stable post-radiation fibrotic/inflammatory change in the left lower lobe, continuing to evolve favorably since the prior study
- Post-surgical changes in the left axilla, stable

---

PART II: BILATERAL BREAST MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE: Bilateral breast MRI performed at 3.0 Tesla with a dedicated breast coil. Pre-contrast T1- and T2-weighted sequences obtained. Dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) series acquired with temporal resolution of approximately 60 seconds per phase over 7 phases following intravenous gadolinium-based contrast administration. Subtraction images, maximum intensity projections (MIP), and kinetic curve analysis reviewed. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) sequences included.

BREAST COMPOSITION: Heterogeneously dense fibroglandular tissue bilaterally (Category C), with post-radiation architectural changes in the left breast upper outer quadrant partially obscuring background fibroglandular signal.

LEFT BREAST:

Surgical Site and Tumor Bed: In the left upper outer quadrant at the 10 o'clock position, approximately 4 cm from the nipple, there is a surgical scar with associated architectural distortion, skin tethering, and seroma/fibrotic change measuring approximately 1.8 x 1.3 cm in greatest dimension. A localization clip is identified at the center of this region. The overall appearance is that of expected post-lumpectomy change. On the dynamic contrast-enhanced sequences, there is a rim of thin, linear, non-nodular enhancement surrounding the surgical bed, with slow progressive kinetics and no washout curve morphology. This pattern is entirely consistent with post-surgical scar enhancement and is not suspicious for residual or recurrent malignancy. There is no new mass, nodular enhancement, or non-mass enhancement in or around the tumor bed.

The remainder of the left breast fibroglandular tissue shows diffuse, symmetric background parenchymal enhancement of mild-to-moderate degree. There is mild diffuse skin thickening of the left breast with mild trabecular thickening, consistent with post-radiation change. No focal skin thickening or nipple retraction beyond expected post-treatment findings.

No new mass lesion is identified in the left breast. No new non-mass enhancement. No suspicious focus on DWI with restricted diffusion outside the expected surgical scar territory.

Left Nipple-Areolar Complex: No suspicious enhancement. No nipple inversion beyond baseline post-treatment findings.

Chest Wall and Pectoral Muscles: The left pectoral muscles are intact without evidence of invasion or edema beyond post-radiation change. No chest wall mass.

RIGHT BREAST:

The right breast demonstrates no suspicious mass, no suspicious non-mass enhancement, and no focal area of restricted diffusion. Background parenchymal enhancement is mild and symmetric. No architectural distortion. No skin thickening. Right nipple-areolar complex is unremarkable.

BILATERAL AXILLAE:

Left Axilla: Post-surgical fibrous changes are present in the left axillary bed, unchanged compared to prior imaging. No pathologically enlarged left axillary lymph nodes. The largest visible node in the left axilla measures approximately 0.9 cm in short axis with a preserved fatty hilum, morphologically benign.

Right Axilla: No pathologically enlarged right axillary lymph nodes. Normal nodal morphology bilaterally.

COMPARISON WITH PRIOR BREAST MRI (POST-NEOADJUVANT, PRE-SURGERY):
The previously identified 1.4 x 1.1 x 0.9 cm residual enhancing mass is no longer visualized as a discrete enhancing lesion. Enhancement at the surgical bed is linear and scar-like, as described above. The previously abnormal left axillary nodes are no longer identified as distinct morphologically abnormal nodes. Overall, there has been further evolution of changes consistent with the interval surgical resection and radiation therapy. No new suspicious finding has developed in either breast since the pre-surgical imaging.

BREAST MRI IMPRESSION:
- Expected post-lumpectomy and post-radiation changes in the left breast upper outer quadrant with no suspicious residual or recurrent enhancing lesion
- No new suspicious mass or non-mass enhancement in either breast
- No pathologic axillary adenopathy bilaterally
- No evidence of local recurrence or new primary malignancy

---

OVERALL IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease on this combined CT and breast MRI surveillance study obtained at approximately 12 months post-surgery, coinciding with completion of all adjuvant systemic therapy.

2. Post-treatment changes in the left breast are stable and expected, including post-lumpectomy architectural distortion, post-radiation skin and parenchymal changes, and benign-appearing left axillary post-surgical soft tissue remodeling.

3. Stable post-radiation fibrotic change in the left lower lung, continuing to resolve favorably with no new pulmonary abnormality.

4. No hepatic, osseous, peritoneal, or soft tissue findings concerning for distant metastatic spread.

Findings communicated to referring oncology team. Continued routine surveillance per institutional and NCCN guidelines is appropriate.
